_title: IEC 60364-5-54:2011+AMD1:2021 — Earthing Arrangements and Protective Conductors
_note: This file covers: choice of earthing system, protective conductor sizing, equipotential bonding, earth electrode design and sizing, and the special requirements for each earthing system type.

earthing_system_types._coding: Two letters: first = supply earth arrangement (T=directly earthed, I=isolated), second = consumer earth arrangement (T=separate electrode, N=connected to neutral)

TN_S.description: Separate N (neutral) and PE (protective earth) conductors throughout the system, from the supply transformer to all consumer equipment.
TN_S.supply_earth: Transformer neutral directly connected to earth at transformer
TN_S.consumer_earth: PE conductor from transformer neutral earth, run alongside distribution cables. Never combined with N.
advantages: No neutral current in PE — no interference with equipment or protective devices
Clean earth reference — best for sensitive electronics and instrumentation
RCDs function reliably — no common-mode noise from neutral current
disadvantages: Requires dedicated PE conductor throughout — higher cable costs
More complex distribution cable (separate N and PE cores)
TN_S.where_used: Preferred for: industrial sites with sensitive instruments or VFDs, medical facilities, data centres, new commercial buildings. Increasingly common in Sub-Saharan Africa for urban commercial supplies.

TN_C.description: A single PEN conductor carries both neutral current and earth function throughout. No separate PE conductor in the distribution network.
TN_C.consumer_earth: PEN conductor serves as both N and PE
TN_C.restriction: NOT PERMITTED for new consumer installations — only for fixed distribution (utility networks). Cannot be used in new buildings per IEC 60364.
TN_C.risk: If PEN conductor breaks, all exposed metal parts of equipment connected to it become live at phase voltage — dangerous and not acceptable
TN_C.where_found: Older utility networks in Europe and some developing countries. Never in new consumer wiring.

TN_C_S.description: PEN conductor in the supply network (utility distribution). At the consumer's main earthing terminal (MET), the PEN is split: neutral continues as N, earth is derived as PE.
TN_C_S.consumer_earth: PE derived from PEN split at MET. PE is separate from N within the consumer's installation.
advantages: Lower installation cost (DNO provides earth via neutral — no separate earth electrode needed in many cases)
Good ADS performance — low earth fault loop impedance because the supply neutral is the return path
Dominant system in UK (as PME), many parts of Europe, Africa, and Asia
disadvantages: Broken PEN upstream = all consumer metalwork at live potential (PME hazard)
Not suitable for certain rural/coastal areas with high soil resistivity or long overhead lines
Cannot use TN-C-S where the supply has significant length of overhead distribution (risk of broken neutral)
TN_C_S.where_used: Dominant in urban Africa (Nigeria, Kenya, Ghana, South Africa), most of Europe. Rural areas often use TT instead due to PEN hazard from long overhead lines.
TN_C_S.PME_caution: In TN-C-S (PME) systems, the PEN conductor earthing points along the distribution route reduce fault loop impedance — BUT also mean that a broken PEN upstream raises the consumer's metalwork to a dangerous voltage. This risk must be assessed for each site.

TT.description: Supply transformer neutral directly earthed (at transformer). Consumer has a completely separate and independent earth electrode.
TT.consumer_earth: Local earth electrode at the consumer's premises — rod, plate, tape, or foundation earth.
advantages: Independent of supply neutral — no PME hazard
Suitable for rural areas with overhead supply lines
Preferred where PEN conductor continuity cannot be guaranteed
disadvantages: Earth fault current limited by electrode resistance — typically too low for overcurrent devices to trip quickly. RCDs are mandatory for all circuits.
Electrode resistance must be regularly tested to ensure it remains within design parameters
More complex to achieve low electrode resistance in rocky or sandy soils
TT.where_used: Rural Africa (where overhead lines dominate supply), older urban installations not yet upgraded, areas without reliable utility earth, caravan parks, marinas, wherever PME hazard is unacceptable

IT.description: Supply neutral is either entirely isolated from earth, or connected to earth through a high impedance (typical 1000–2000Ω). No direct path for earth fault current on first fault.
IT.consumer_earth: All exposed conductive parts connected to earth electrode via PE, but no low-impedance path to neutral.
IT.first_fault_behaviour: On first fault to earth, the fault current is limited to leakage through supply impedance — typically < 1A. System continues operating. IMD gives alarm.
IT.second_fault_behaviour: Creates a fault loop similar to TN between the two faulted points. Must disconnect within the times from Table 41.1.
advantages: Highest continuity of supply — first fault does not cause disconnection
Critical for: operating theatres (Group 2 medical), semiconductor fabrication, offshore platforms, arc furnaces
IT.where_used: Group 2 medical locations (per IEC 60364-7-710), industrial processes that cannot tolerate shutdown on fault, offshore oil and gas

main_earthing_terminal_MET.definition: The central connection point in the installation where all earthing and bonding conductors are connected. Every installation must have a MET (or MEB — Main Equipotential Bonding terminal).
connections_to_MET: Earth electrode conductor (TT systems, or TN-C-S supplement)
Exposed-conductive-parts of all equipment via CPCs
Extraneous conductive parts — incoming metallic services: water, gas, oil, other structural metalwork
Lightning protection system equipotential bonding (where LPS is installed)
Functional earth where required (IT, medical, data)
main_earthing_terminal_MET.disconnection_facility: The connection between the MET and the earth electrode must be accessible and disconnectable for testing (without disconnecting the installation earth). Usually achieved with a disconnectable link or test clamp.

protective_conductor_sizing._reference: IEC 60364-5-54 Table 54.2 (cross-section of protective conductors in same cable as phase conductors)
protective_conductor_sizing._basis: The CPC must be able to carry the earth fault current for the duration of protective device operation without thermal damage (adiabatic check). See fault-current.json. The table gives minimum sizes based on the adiabatic check.

minimum_CPC_cross_sections._title: Minimum CPC cross-section as a function of phase conductor cross-section
minimum_CPC_cross_sections._condition: CPC of the same conductor material as phase conductor
minimum_CPC_cross_sections.S_phase_50_and_above: S_CPC = S_phase / 2 (rounded up to standard size)
minimum_CPC_cross_sections.note: These are the minimum values. Where the adiabatic check (fault-current.json) gives a larger CPC requirement, use the calculated value. The table gives minimum starting points, not the absolute answer.

CPE_in_same_cable.description: When the CPC is a core within the cable (multi-core cable or T&E), it benefits from the same thermal environment as the phase conductor.
CPE_in_same_cable.sizing: Use minimum CPC table above. Adiabatic check rarely requires larger than table minimum for standard residential and commercial circuits.

separate_CPC.description: Where the CPC is a separate conductor (not in the same cable), the thermal environment may differ.

bare_copper_cpc_on_tray.note: Higher k than insulated copper (115) — slightly smaller size may be used but for convenience use same table values

bare_copper_buried.minimum_size_buried: If buried in ground, minimum 16mm² copper for durability regardless of adiabatic check
bare_copper_buried.note: Corrosion and mechanical damage dictate minimum size for buried CPCs

steel_conduit_as_CPC.note: Steel conduit can serve as CPC but k value is low — often requires larger cross-section conduit to satisfy adiabatic check. Verify by calculation for each circuit.

colour_coding.PEN_conductor: Green-yellow with blue marking at terminations, or blue with green-yellow at terminations
colour_coding.bare_CPC: Green-yellow tape every 150mm, or at ends and junctions

earth_electrodes._purpose: Establish an electrical connection to the general mass of earth. Required in TT systems. Recommended as supplementary in TN-C-S (PME) systems where PEN failure is possible.

earth_rod_or_pipe.description: Copper-bonded steel or stainless steel rod, 16–25mm diameter, driven vertically into the ground.
earth_rod_or_pipe.typical_length: 1.2m to 3m. Can be coupled rods driven to 6m+ in high-resistivity soil.

approximate_resistance.formula: R ≈ ρ / (2π × L) where ρ = soil resistivity (Ω·m), L = rod length (m)

horizontal_tape_or_strip.description: Copper tape or strip buried horizontally, typically in a ring around the building or as a long radial.
horizontal_tape_or_strip.typical_applications: Perimeter earth electrodes for large buildings, where soil is too rocky for rods

foundation_earth_electrode.description: Steel reinforcement bar of building foundation connected to the earthing system. Provides large contact area with soil.
foundation_earth_electrode.advantage: Very low earth resistance — often < 1Ω for large buildings. Also provides effective bonding for the whole building structure.
foundation_earth_electrode.connection: Requires good electrical continuity of rebar — mechanical coupling at laps may not be sufficient. Specify welded or bonded connections at design stage.
foundation_earth_electrode.use: Standard practice for new multi-storey buildings. Most effective and durable electrode type.

earth_plate.description: Copper or steel plate (minimum 500mm × 500mm × 2mm copper) buried vertically.
earth_plate.use: Limited space situations, or supplement to rod electrodes

resistance_requirements.TT_systems_general: Ra × IΔn ≤ 50V. For 30mA RCD: Ra ≤ 1667Ω. For 100mA: Ra ≤ 500Ω. For 300mA: Ra ≤ 167Ω.
resistance_requirements.TT_maximum_practical: 50Ω — most installations can achieve this without special measures. Ensures RCDs remain effective.
resistance_requirements.TN_C_S_supplementary: No specific limit — supplementary electrode in TN-C-S improves resilience if PEN fails. Target < 100Ω as good practice.
resistance_requirements.lightning_protection: IEC 62305 — Ra ≤ 10Ω preferred for LPS earth electrodes. For low soil resistivity ≤ 1Ω is achievable.
resistance_requirements.IT_systems: No specific earth electrode resistance limit — the system continues on first fault. Target < 50Ω for effective second fault detection.

measuring_electrode_resistance.method: Fall-of-potential (3-point) method using an earth electrode tester
measuring_electrode_resistance.procedure: Drive a current spike 30–40m from the electrode under test. Drive a potential spike at 62% of this distance. Measure resistance between electrode and potential spike with test current flowing.
measuring_electrode_resistance.when_to_test: At installation, and periodically (every 5 years for critical installations per IEC 60364-6)
measuring_electrode_resistance.note: Test in DRY conditions — soil resistivity is lower when wet, masking high-resistance poor electrode. Worst-case is dry season. Record both wet and dry season measurements.

main_bonding.purpose: Connect all metallic services entering the building (water, gas, oil, telecommunications, structural metalwork) to the MET. Ensures all metalwork is at the same earth potential.
connections_required: Metallic water service pipe — at point of entry into building
Metallic gas service pipe — at point of entry
Metallic oil supply pipe — at point of entry
Structural steel framework — column or beam adjacent to MET
Central heating metallic pipework — if not already connected via exposed-conductive-parts of heating equipment

sizing.typical_commercial: 10mm² to 25mm² copper (for large buildings with high fault levels)
sizing.formula: Minimum S_bonding = S_phase / 2 of the largest circuit conductor in the installation, but not less than 6mm² copper
sizing.note: Where MET is connected to supplementary earth electrode, size bonding conductor for full earth fault current flow if needed.

supplementary_bonding.purpose: Additional local bonding between simultaneously accessible conductive parts where ADS time requirement cannot otherwise be met, or in specific high-risk locations.
required_in: Bathrooms (IEC 60364-7-701) — between all exposed conductive parts and extraneous conductive parts within reach (bath, shower tray, exposed pipework, metallic window frames within reach)
Agricultural buildings (IEC 60364-7-705) — between metallic structure, water pipes, and livestock floor level
Swimming pool surrounds — between metallic ladders, handrails, pool coping metalwork
Any location where ADS check fails and supplementary bonding is the chosen remedy
supplementary_bonding.verification: R ≤ 50V / Ia (where Ia is the instantaneous trip current of the protective device) — confirms contact voltage is limited
